articles.title: Artifacts dating back 400k years, found in cave, show 'complex and rich'
articles.summary: Article URL: https://www.cnn.com/2026/06/29/science/israel-prehistoric-cave-scli-intl Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48723565 Points: 1 # Comments: 2
articles.content:
Archaeologists in Israel have discovered a cave used by pre-Neanderthal human-like creatures who lived as long as 400,000 years ago, making it one of only a handful of sites from the little-known period accessible to scientists.

Located on the outskirts of a town called Fureidis in northern Israel, the cave was due to be affected by construction work when archaeologists decided to investigate it.

Researchers in the 1970s originally thought the cave was used around 200,000 years ago, archaeologist Kobi Vardi of the Israel Antiquities Authority told CNN on Thursday.

However, Vardi and his colleague Ron Shimelmitz, an associate professor in archaeology at the University of Haifa, have now discovered that it was inhabited up to 400,000 years ago.

They determined this after finding flint tools, such as hand axes, scrapers and blades, that are characteristic of the Acheulo-Yabrudian culture of pre-Neanderthal hominins who lived in the area at the time.

Vardi told CNN that “it was a big surprise” to find that the cave was much older than previously thought. It would have been used by members of the Acheulo-Yabrudian culture, who inhabited the Levant, or the Near East, around 400,000-250,000 years ago.

The team also found bones from animals such as fallow deer and gazelle.

This suggests large groups of hominins lived together in the caves, hunting wild animals and using fire, “suggesting complex and rich camp life,” Shimelmitz said in a statement published by Israel’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs on June 11.

However, to date no significant human remains have been found in any of the caves that have been discovered from this period.

The cave in Fureidis is “a unique site of global importance,” Shimelmitz said in the statement.

“This time capsule belongs to a unique period at the end of the Lower Paleolithic era, just before Neanderthals and modern humans became dominant and spread across many regions,” he said.

“Only a handful of sites from this important phase have been uncovered in Israel and the wider Levant, and most of them are inaccessible for research,” Shimelmitz added.

The team presented their findings to the construction company, and a road bridge was built to save the cave and keep it accessible for further investigation.

Vardi said researchers are planning extensive studies at the site that will probably take several years.

“Our big hope in the excavation of these caves is that maybe we’ll find hominin remains,” he said. “We’re very anxious to meet them.”

‘Transform our understanding’

Armando Falcucci, a lecturer in palaeolithic archaeology at the University of Southampton, England, who was not involved in the study, told CNN that the find adds to our understanding of a period of human history that tends to receive limited attention.

“What makes this find valuable is that it pulls the spotlight back to a much earlier and equally pivotal window (roughly 400,000 to 200,000 years ago); a period of profound behavioural and technological change across both Africa and Eurasia in its own right, including the shift toward intensive, repeated use of caves as central places in the landscape,” he told CNN on Wednesday. He stressed that cave deposits from this period “are extremely rare in the region.”

“The evidence for intensive use of fire at this site is particularly significant,” Falcucci added. “It is during this period, and at sites of exactly this kind, that habitual, controlled use of fire becomes archaeologically visible, marking a major behavioural threshold in human evolution.”

Catriona Pickard, a professor of prehistory and archaeometry, and head of archaeology, at the University of Edinburgh in Scotland, who was also not involved in the research, said the findings “provide rare insights into the material culture and lifeways of early hominins, and as such, the site has the potential to transform our understanding of the Lower Palaeolithic in the Levant.”

Sign up for CNN’s Wonder Theory science newsletter. Explore the universe with news on fascinating discoveries, scientific advancements and more.

articles.title: Old vs. New Chevy Blazer Crash Test Shows How Much Safety Has Improved
articles.summary: Article URL: https://www.thedrive.com/news/1996-vs-2026-chevy-blazer-crash-test-shows-how-much-safety-has-improved-since-the-90s Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48723549 Points: 1 # Comments: 0
articles.content:
In 2009, the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety crashed a 1959 Chevrolet Bel Air into a then-new 2009 Chevy Malibu to prove how far automotive safety had come. That vintage cars were fragile death traps and not tough tanks proved hard for some people to accept, but given their lack of safety features and the limited understanding of crash performance before the implementation of federal safety standards, that makes sense. Airbags and crumple zones made a huge difference, but that doesn’t mean safety improvements have stopped.

The IIHS recently conducted another new vs. old crash test to prove that. This time, it pitted a 1996 Chevy S-10 Blazer against a 2026 Blazer to show how much its testing program has helped improve car safety over the past 30 years. The cars were crashed head-to-head under the same parameters as the IIHS’s moderate overlap front test, which normally involves a single car being run into a stationary barrier at 40 mph. I can’t tell what was more painful: watching that mint S-10 Blazer get destroyed in the name of science, or the existential crisis brought on by the realization that said Blazer is, in fact, three decades old.

The front end of the new Blazer absorbed most of the impact, keeping the cabin intact, which would have allowed a real-life driver to walk away with only minor “bumps and bruises,” according to an IIHS press release. In contrast, the 1996 S-10 Blazer’s was compressed, pushing the dashboard and steering column into the crash-test dummy’s lap. Instead of softening the blow, the airbag hit the dummy in the chin, pushing its head back with such force that it detached.

Both SUVs performed as expected. The 1996 Blazer earned the lowest “Poor” rating in the moderate overlap front test when new, and the 2026 Blazer received the highest “Good” rating. However, it hasn’t yet undergone the updated version of the test introduced in 2022, which also looks at rear-passenger safety.

The IIHS has been testing cars since 1995, using data from insurance companies to find crash scenarios not covered by federal regulations that tend to have high rates of injuries and deaths in the real world, and those same insurers’ money to conduct the tests. It’s dropped or added tests over the years to keep automakers on their toes, and also expanded the testing regime to cover safety-related features like headlights and automatic emergency braking systems.

The nonprofit estimates that safety improvements resulting from its crash tests saved 48,352 lives between 1999 and 2024. That’s based on a comparison of real-world fatality rates for cars and trucks rated “Good” and those with the lower ratings of “Acceptable,” “Marginal,” and “Poor.” The IIHS also says safety improvements saved $538 billion, citing U.S. Department of Transportation data. That’s a nearly 900-fold return on investment from the $600 million insurance companies spent on IIHS crash testing during that time.

With continued funding from the insurance industry, the IIHS isn’t resting on its laurels. It’s implementing an updated whiplash test and evaluating larger commercial trucks—something the federal government refuses to do.

articles.title: Firefly Aerospace Operates Nvidia Jetson in Lunar Orbit for First Time
articles.summary: Article URL: https://www.narracomm.com/firefly-aerospace-successfully-operates-nvidia-jetson-in-lunar-orbit-for-first-time/ Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48723546 Points: 2 # Comments: 0
articles.content:
Firefly Aerospace has achieved a milestone in space-based computing, announcing that its Blue Ghost lunar lander successfully operated an NVIDIA Jetson platform in lunar orbit.

According to NVIDIA’s official newsroom, the deployment marks the first time a Jetson system has functioned in lunar orbit, demonstrating edge AI capabilities in the harsh environment of cislunar space.

The hardware handled real-time data processing tasks during the mission, leveraging Jetson’s power-efficient architecture for onboard inference and autonomy.

Key Technical Details:

  • Jetson platform operated successfully aboard Firefly’s Blue Ghost lander.
  • Demonstrated edge computing and AI processing in lunar orbit conditions.
  • Supports advanced autonomy, sensor fusion, and real-time decision-making without constant Earth-based relay.

This development aligns with broader industry efforts to push high-performance, low-power compute (such as NVIDIA’s Jetson line) beyond low-Earth orbit.

Traditional space systems have relied on radiation-hardened processors with lower performance; Jetson’s deployment signals improving viability for commercial lunar missions requiring onboard AI.

Context and Implications


Firefly Aerospace, a Texas-based company backed by investors including Hedgewood and others, continues to advance its lunar logistics capabilities.

The Blue Ghost mission contributes to NASA’s Commercial Lunar Payload Services (CLPS) program, with successful Jetson operation potentially lowering costs and latency for future payloads needing autonomous navigation, hazard avoidance, or scientific data analysis.

NVIDIA has positioned Jetson for expanding roles in aerospace, robotics, and autonomous systems. This lunar milestone follows terrestrial and orbital precedents and could accelerate adoption in deep-space applications where bandwidth to Earth is limited.

No financial terms or additional mission payload details were disclosed in today’s announcement. Firefly and NVIDIA are expected to provide further performance metrics in coming weeks.

articles.title: Lawmaking as Memorial: Named Laws in the UK
articles.summary: Article URL: https://consoc.org.uk/blog-named-laws/ Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48723517 Points: 1 # Comments: 0
articles.content:
In recent years, Parliament has increasingly enacted ‘named laws’: legislation framed in response to, and in commemoration of, high-profile individuals or groups who have suffered a harm that the new law is intended to prevent or address. Named laws, also commonly referred to as ‘nominative laws’ or ‘apostrophe laws’, may provide a vehicle for grieving families to honour their loved one and to ensure specific legislative change. They may also signal the social salience of a new issue. Such laws are commonly criminal in nature, as the original harm suffered was often caused by criminality of some kind, but named legislation can also be civil or regulatory.

Recent examples of high-profile named laws that the UK Parliament has passed include:

  • Martyn’s Law (The Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act 2025) — enacted in honour of Martyn Hett, a victim of the Manchester Arena terrorist attack. The Act requires public premises to enhance security and preparedness for terrorist threats.
  • Awaab’s Law (Hazards in Social Housing (Prescribed Requirements) (England) Regulations 2025) — enacted in honour of Awaab Ishak, a two-year old boy who died in 2020 from a respiratory condition caused by exposure to mould. The Regulations require social landlords in England to investigate and remedy specific damp, mould, and emergency hazards within strict timeframes.
  • Jade’s Law (2024) — refers to a provision incorporated into the Victim and Prisoners Act 2024 which provides for the automatic suspension of parental responsibility where a parent is convicted of killing the child’s other parent. The provision is named in honour of Jade Ward, who was murdered by her former partner in 2021.
  • Zach’s Law (2023) — refers to a provision in the Online Safety Act 2023 which criminalises the intentional transmission of flashing images (including GIFs) to a person with epilepsy with the intent to trigger a seizure. The provision is named in honour of Zach Eagling, a young boy with epilepsy who was targeted by trolls during a charity walk.
  • Harper’s Law (2022) — refers to a provision in the Police, Crime, Sentencing and Courts Act 2022 which mandates a life sentence for those who unlawfully kill an emergency worker while committing a crime. The provision is named after PC Andrew Harper, who was killed in the line of duty in 2019.
  • The Hillsborough Law (The Public Office (Accountability) Bill), which is still before Parliament, can also be classified as a named law: it proposes a new statutory duty of candour intended to guard against the kind of widespread state cover-up witnessed in the aftermath of the Hillsborough disaster, and it is the product of decades-long campaigning by victims’ families.

At their best, laws of this kind demonstrate the importance of democratic participation in lawmaking. They honour a named victim while also giving rise to necessary and effective legislative reform. However, named laws have also been strenuously critiqued, both in the UK and elsewhere – most notably in the US. Below, I explore the potential benefits of legislating in this manner, as well as the potential risks, and the criticisms of this approach.

Legacy, legitimacy and labelling

Named laws are often associated with a heightened democratic legitimacy. They typically emerge from sustained campaigning, public pressure and media attention, and provide a clear example of the wider public shaping the law-making process. In this sense, the naming itself signals democratic listening. As Irina Fanarraga notes, ‘the growing trend of nominative lawmaking equips ‘ordinary’ citizens with the agency to make substantive, meaningful, change on issues that matter to them, especially in relation to public health or wellbeing, helping citizens to set political agendas and to produce meaningful social and political change.’[1]
Furthermore, named laws can serve as a poignant form of memorialisation for an individual, or a group of individuals.[2] In the UK, the proposed Hillsborough Law illustrates this point. It not only operates as a reminder of the damage and trauma inflicted by state-sanctioned cover-ups in the aftermath of the disaster, but is also intended to stand as a lasting legacy for those that died. In this way, the name of the law acts as a safeguard against institutional amnesia and future repeal.

On a more practical note, named laws enhance accessibility – a key component in the enactment of ‘good’ legislation. Attaching an individual’s name to a piece of legislation generally makes the content of the law easier to remember and reference, particularly for the public and non-specialists. For example, people are likely to remember that Martyn’s Law relates to one of the young victims of the Manchester Arena bombing, helping them understand what the statute does and why. In turn, this makes the law easier for the media to report on, which further increases public awareness. Ultimately, if people understand what a law does and why it exists, they are more likely to comply with it – enhancing its practical effectiveness.
Constitutionally speaking, this is important, as the Office of Parliamentary Counsel (the specialist lawyers responsible for drafting legislation) has identified accessibility as one of the hallmarks of ‘good’ legislation. This is particularly significant when the named law is criminal in nature, as the rule of law requires that criminal statutes must be accessible, intelligible, clear and predictable.

Symbolism, scrutiny and sentencing: The risks of named laws

On the other hand, enacting named laws carries a significant degree of jeopardy for a variety of reasons. Such laws risk becoming pieces of symbolic legislation rather than tangibly impacting legal or political culture. They can be enacted simply in response to events: placating, and sometimes unfortunately empty, political gestures. As Fanarraga explains, ‘sponsors of apostrophe laws rely on them as grand gestures meant to placate an unruly or emotional public. Lawmakers are looking to send a message to their constituents while at the same time being fully aware that, if passed, the laws will have little to no real practical impact’.[3] Symbolic law-making is not uncommon in the UK; notably, in times of emergency or acute crisis, governments often resort to primary legislation to signal that they are in control of an issue to the wider public. Statutes such as the Dangerous Dogs Act 1991 and the Aggravated Vehicle-Taking Act 1992 are pertinent examples of symbolic law-making of this kind. Not only are symbolic laws often reactionary and knee-jerk, but commonly unnecessary and ineffective in practice.

Jarvis, Lister and Powell link this kind of law-making to the increasingly populist style of contemporary British politics. In their view, the spread of nominative laws is connected to two wider trends: ‘first, is the increasingly visible agency of ‘ordinary’ people within mainstream British politics today. Second, is the instrumentalisation of citizens and their experiences by policy makers and legislators for political gain’.[4] These trends have influenced the way in which Parliament functions, incentivising politicians not only to ‘reflect and refer to ‘ordinary’ people and publics, and crucially, to be seen to reflect and refer to ‘ordinary’ people and publics’.[5] In this sense, named laws can become an especially powerful tool for signalling responsiveness – demonstrating to the public that something is being done, and that they are being listened to.

Linked to this, symbolic laws can exploit public emotion and thereby facilitate legislative manipulation. By attaching a bill to a named, headline-making victim and case, the likelihood of it passing through Parliament unopposed or with little amendment may increase. Consequently, a politician may cynically support a named bill through its parliamentary stages, leveraging the cause of the named victim for long-term political gain. In the US context, evidence of this kind of exploitation has been well documented. Indeed, one study suggests that over half of the named laws introduced in a given period had already appeared without reference to their named victim before they were so attached and then passed.[6] Such findings suggest that legislators may strategically associate particular victims or incidents with pre-existing draft bills in order to enhance their chances of enactment, although this US phenomenon has yet to be so widely replicated in the UK.

The emotion attached to named laws can also undermine the process of legislative scrutiny, ultimately jeopardising the quality of the law that is enacted. Parliamentarians may be reluctant to oppose or to actively seek to amend a bill of this kind for fear of appearing unjust or unaffected by the human story behind the legislative proposals. As another study explains, ‘it is much harder to oppose or question legislation when such legislation is proposed as a form of justice for victims of tragic harm or wrongdoing’. In practice, this legislation can close down parliamentary debate and prevent MPs and Peers from carrying out their proper constitutional function, then. As I have explained in detail elsewhere, when the process of legislative scrutiny is subverted, various benefits are lost. Perhaps most notably in this context, the technical quality of laws can suffer – the necessity, accessibility, coherence and clarity of a bill may be undermined if parliamentarians are reluctant to critically engage during its passage.

Finally, and perhaps most obviously, named laws may distort principles of justice and contribute to uneven sentencing outcomes. Where emotional framing constrains scrutiny and discourages amendment, the risk is not merely symbolic: it may culminate in substantively distorted and uneven sentencing outcomes. As a result of the campaigns surrounding named laws, comparable criminal conduct can attract different sentences depending on the context of the offence, or the identity of the victim. For example, following the passage of Harper’s Law (noted above), an individual who kills an emergency service worker who is in the line of duty is now subject to a mandatory life sentence, a penalty ordinarily reserved for murder. However, unlike murder, those sentenced under Harper’s Law may not have intended to kill (the necessary mens rea requirement for murder); the severity of the sentence stems from who was killed, rather than how the offender acted. Notably, judges already have the discretion to impose life sentences for manslaughter in appropriate cases, raising the question of whether mandatory sentencing provisions were necessary.

As Baroness Kennedy has highlighted, victim-led sentencing of this kind creates a hierarchy of ‘worthy’ victims. Although many of the cases that prompt named laws are undeniably tragic, it is a dangerous precedent to differentiate between victims when determining a criminal punishment. Ultimately, it is difficult to reconcile this approach with the rule of law.

Conclusion

I’ve not sought to offer an exhaustive account of named laws and their consequences here. Instead, I’ve aimed to highlight this growing legislative trend and to encourage wider debate about what it means for law-making, scrutiny and justice. Ultimately, if named laws are to remain a feature of modern legislation, greater emphasis may need to be placed on ensuring that the symbolic power of naming does not displace the ordinary safeguards of scrutiny, accountability and robust parliamentary debate.

Kate Dewsnip.

Kate is a Graduate Teaching Fellow and PhD candidate at the University of Liverpool School of Law and Social Justice. She is a contributing writer for the Constitution Society.
The Constitution Society is committed to the promotion of informed debate and is politically impartial. Any views expressed in this article are the personal views of the author and not those of The Constitution Society.

[1] Irina Fanarraga, ‘What’s in a Name? An Empirical Analysis of Apostrophe Laws’ (2020) Criminology, Criminal Justice, Law & Society 21(3) 39–68.

[2] ibid

[3] ibid

[4] Lee Jarvis, Michael Lister and Alex Powell, ‘Stop in the Law of the Name! Nominative Lawmaking, Populism and Justice’ (2025) The Political Quarterly, 5

[5] ibid

[6] Fanarranga (n 1)
